episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [The Marginalian] The Force and the Flower: Poet and Potter M.C. Richards on Creativity and [STAT News] Opinion: The podcast telling the stories behind Ambien, Ozempic, EpiPens, and other game-changing drugs, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How should Canada regulate digital platforms to protect rural and Indigenous cultural storytelling in the absence of platform accountability structures? Topics include: Platform algorithms as non-neutral distribution infrastructure that shapes what gets created, Data portability and creator sovereignty as components of duty of care frameworks, Community-owned alternative infrastructure (newsletters, podcasts, local archives) as durable alternatives to platform dependency, AI's role in cultural production: generation vs. connective discovery for community storytellers, Risks of overregulation creating automated content moderation harms to marginalized creators.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Central Cariboo Arts & Culture Society

articles.title: Fight Online Harms: Canada Needs a Duty of Care
articles.summary: â ï¸ Big Tech has free rein in Canada. Every democracy has acted, except Canada. AI-era online harms are no joke. Demand Duty of Care now! ð¨ð¦  https://openmedia.org/FightOnlineHarms

discussion.central_question: How should Canada regulate digital platforms to protect rural and Indigenous cultural storytelling in the absence of platform accountability structures?
discussion.riley_position: Platform regulation alone won't solve the distribution problem for rural creators; communities should build direct audience relationships and owned infrastructure independent of platforms, using AI as connective tissue rather than production capacity.
riley_key_evidence: Algorithms systematically favor emotionally provocative content over carefully produced oral histories, creating a structural disadvantage for community storytellers
Email lists and direct podcast feeds bypass algorithmic suppression and cannot be taken away by platforms
AI search tools with data sovereignty protections (like searching oral history footage by plain language) could solve archive interpretation bottlenecks without extracting community data
discussion.casey_position: Canada's regulatory gap creates measurable harms to cultural diversity; a duty of care framework with procedural protections and data portability rights could level the playing field while platform power continuously expands.
casey_key_evidence: Meta blocked Canadian news entirely rather than pay link fees under the Online News Act, demonstrating real harms from regulatory gaps
The UK has the Online Safety Act, Australia has platform duty of care, and the EU has the Digital Services Act—Canada has none
AI-generated content automation is widening the gap between industrial content factories and community producers, making the regulatory timeline urgent
discussion.resolution: They converged on a both/and position: communities need both federal duty of care regulation and immediate local action (owned infrastructure, direct relationships), with platforms used as 'doors, not houses.' Casey conceded regulation moves slowly; Riley conceded platform reach is genuinely valuable and shouldn't be abandoned.
topics_covered: Platform algorithms as non-neutral distribution infrastructure that shapes what gets created
Data portability and creator sovereignty as components of duty of care frameworks
Community-owned alternative infrastructure (newsletters, podcasts, local archives) as durable alternatives to platform dependency
AI's role in cultural production: generation vs. connective discovery for community storytellers
Risks of overregulation creating automated content moderation harms to marginalized creators
